options: Positive TSI antibodies and exophthalmos
Positive anti-TPO antibodies and goiter
Elevated TSH with decreased T4 levels
Weight gain with cold intolerance
q: A nurse is caring for a patient newly diagnosed with Graves disease. Which assessment finding does the nurse expect to differentiate this condition from Hashimoto thyroiditis?
rationale: Graves disease is differentiated from Hashimoto thyroiditis by the presence of thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) that mimic TSH, driving excess thyroid hormone production and causing exophthalmos (bulging eyes). Hashimoto thyroiditis is characterized by anti-TPO antibodies that destroy thyroid tissue, leading to hypothyroidism rather than hyperthyroidism.

options: Heat intolerance and diaphoresis
Positive anti-TPO antibodies with gradual-onset hypothyroidism
Tremor and tachycardia at rest
Exophthalmos and lid lag
q: A nurse is assessing two patients: one with Graves disease and one with Hashimoto thyroiditis. Which finding is unique to the patient with Hashimoto thyroiditis?
rationale: Positive anti-TPO antibodies with gradual-onset hypothyroidism is the hallmark of Hashimoto thyroiditis, an autoimmune condition causing progressive thyroid destruction. While both Graves and Hashimoto are autoimmune, Graves disease presents with hyperthyroid symptoms such as tremor, tachycardia, and exophthalmos driven by TSI antibodies.

options: Apply ice packs to the orbits to reduce swelling
Administer levothyroxine as prescribed
Elevate the head of bed and apply lubricating eye drops
Prepare the patient for immediate thyroidectomy
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Graves disease who reports worsening eye protrusion. Which intervention should the nurse prioritize FIRST?
rationale: For a patient with Graves-related exophthalmos, the nurse should first elevate the head of bed to reduce periorbital edema and apply lubricating eye drops to protect the cornea from drying and ulceration. This is a priority comfort and safety intervention; ice packs are not indicated, and immediate thyroidectomy is not the first response to eye protrusion.

options: Hashimoto thyroiditis causes temporary inflammation that resolves after treatment
Antithyroid drugs will cure the autoimmune process within one year
radioactive iodine therapy will restore normal thyroid function
Autoimmune destruction of thyroid tissue is progressive and irreversible, requiring lifelong levothyroxine replacement
q: A nurse is teaching a patient newly diagnosed with Hashimoto thyroiditis. The patient asks why lifelong medication is needed. Which response by the nurse is MOST accurate?
rationale: Hashimoto thyroiditis involves progressive, irreversible autoimmune destruction of thyroid tissue by anti-TPO antibodies, resulting in permanent hypothyroidism. Because the thyroid gland cannot regenerate, lifelong levothyroxine replacement therapy is required to maintain normal T4 levels and metabolic function.

options: Elevated TSI, suppressed TSH, and elevated T3 and T4
Elevated anti-TPO, elevated TSH, and decreased T4
Normal TSH with elevated cortisol
Suppressed TSH with normal T3 and T4
q: A nurse is reviewing the chart of a patient with suspected Graves disease. Which combination of laboratory findings confirms the diagnosis?
rationale: Graves disease is confirmed by elevated TSI antibodies (which mimic TSH and stimulate the thyroid), suppressed TSH (due to negative feedback from excess hormone), and elevated T3 and T4. This triad of lab findings distinguishes Graves from other causes of thyroid dysfunction.

options: Low TSH and elevated T4
Elevated TSH and decreased T4
Normal TSH and elevated T3
Suppressed TSH and suppressed T4
q: A nurse is reviewing labs for a patient with suspected hypothyroidism. Which result pattern is consistent with primary hypothyroidism?
rationale: In primary hypothyroidism, the thyroid gland underproduces hormones, so T4 is decreased. The pituitary gland compensates by increasing TSH secretion in an attempt to stimulate the failing thyroid. This inverse TSH-T4 relationship is the hallmark of primary thyroid disease.

options: Elevated TSH, elevated T4, elevated cholesterol
Normal TSH, normal T4, elevated glucose
Suppressed TSH, elevated T3 and T4, decreased cholesterol
Elevated TSH, decreased T3, normal T4
q: A nurse is interpreting lab results for a patient with hyperthyroidism. Which lab pattern does the nurse expect?
rationale: In hyperthyroidism, the thyroid overproduces T3 and T4, which suppresses TSH through negative feedback. Excess thyroid hormone accelerates lipid metabolism, resulting in decreased cholesterol. This lab triad of suppressed TSH, elevated T3/T4, and decreased cholesterol is characteristic of hyperthyroidism.

options: Hashimoto thyroiditis
Primary adrenal insufficiency
Myxedema coma
Graves disease or another cause of hyperthyroidism
q: A nurse is caring for a patient whose TSH is 0.1 mIU/L and T4 is 18.5 mcg/dL. The nurse recognizes this pattern is consistent with which condition?
rationale: A suppressed TSH of 0.1 mIU/L combined with an elevated T4 of 18.5 mcg/dL indicates hyperthyroidism, most commonly caused by Graves disease. The excess T4 suppresses TSH through negative feedback on the pituitary gland.

options: The provider will likely increase the levothyroxine dose because the elevated TSH indicates inadequate replacement
The provider will likely decrease the dose because TSH is too high
No dose change is needed since this is within normal range
The medication will be discontinued and replaced with methimazole
q: A nurse is reviewing labs for a patient on levothyroxine therapy. The TSH is 12 mIU/L. Which action should the nurse anticipate?
rationale: An elevated TSH of 12 mIU/L in a patient on levothyroxine indicates the current dose is inadequate, as the pituitary is producing excess TSH trying to stimulate more thyroid hormone production. The provider will likely increase the levothyroxine dose and recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks.

options: Graves disease with early remission
Subclinical or early Hashimoto thyroiditis progressing toward overt hypothyroidism
Thyroid storm
Pheochromocytoma
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with elevated TSH and elevated anti-TPO antibodies but normal T4. The nurse understands this presentation is MOST consistent with which condition?
rationale: Elevated TSH with positive anti-TPO antibodies but normal T4 represents subclinical or early Hashimoto thyroiditis. The autoimmune process has begun destroying thyroid tissue, causing compensatory TSH elevation, but T4 levels remain normal for now. This pattern typically progresses to overt hypothyroidism over time.

options: I can take it with my morning coffee and breakfast for convenience
I should take it at bedtime with a glass of milk
I will take it on an empty stomach, 30 to 60 minutes before breakfast, at the same time every day
I only need to take it when I feel fatigued or cold
q: A nurse is teaching a patient about levothyroxine therapy. Which statement by the patient indicates correct understanding?
rationale: Levothyroxine must be taken on an empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before breakfast, at the same time daily for consistent absorption. Food, calcium, iron, and antacids interfere with absorption. Taking it with coffee, milk, or meals significantly reduces bioavailability.

options: Administer acetaminophen and reassess in 4 hours
Encourage increased oral fluid intake
Document the findings and continue the medication
Hold the methimazole and notify the provider to obtain a STAT WBC and differential, as these symptoms may indicate agranulocytosis
q: A nurse is caring for a patient taking methimazole who reports a sore throat and fever of 101.4 degrees Fahrenheit. What is the nurse's PRIORITY action?
rationale: Sore throat and fever in a patient taking methimazole are warning signs of agranulocytosis, a life-threatening drop in WBC count. The nurse must hold the methimazole immediately and notify the provider to obtain a STAT WBC with differential, as continuing the medication could be fatal.

options: Maintain radiation safety precautions including limited contact time, distance, and proper disposal of body fluids
Administer the I-131 with a full meal to prevent nausea
Start levothyroxine simultaneously with I-131
Monitor for immediate anaphylaxis for the first 15 minutes only
q: A nurse is preparing to administer radioactive iodine (I-131) to a patient with Graves disease. Which safety precaution is MOST important for the nurse to implement?
rationale: When administering radioactive iodine (I-131), the nurse must implement radiation safety precautions including limiting contact time, maintaining distance, and ensuring proper disposal of body fluids. The patient emits radiation after treatment, posing a risk to healthcare workers and visitors, especially pregnant women.

options: PTU works faster than methimazole in all situations
PTU is preferred during the first trimester of pregnancy and in thyroid storm because it also blocks peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion
PTU has fewer side effects than methimazole
PTU is less hepatotoxic than methimazole
q: A nurse is caring for a patient taking PTU who asks why this medication was chosen over methimazole. Which response by the nurse is accurate?
rationale: PTU is preferred over methimazole during the first trimester of pregnancy (due to lower teratogenicity) and in thyroid storm because PTU has the unique ability to block peripheral conversion of T4 to the more active T3, providing faster symptomatic relief.

options: Report any changes in urine color immediately
Monitor daily weights and restrict sodium intake
Report sore throat, fever, or mouth sores immediately as these may indicate a dangerously low WBC count from agranulocytosis
Avoid sun exposure due to photosensitivity
q: A nurse is teaching a patient prescribed methimazole about the importance of monitoring for adverse effects. Which instruction should the nurse include?
rationale: Agranulocytosis is the most dangerous adverse effect of methimazole, causing a critically low WBC count that leaves the patient vulnerable to life-threatening infections. The patient must report sore throat, fever, or mouth sores immediately, as these may be the first signs of this potentially fatal complication.

options: SSKI to block thyroid hormone release
aspirin to reduce the high fever
levothyroxine to stabilize thyroid levels
PTU to block new thyroid hormone synthesis, followed by SSKI at least one hour later
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in thyroid storm. Which medication should be administered FIRST?
rationale: In thyroid storm, PTU must be administered FIRST to block new thyroid hormone synthesis and peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion. SSKI (potassium iodide) is given at least one hour later to block hormone release, because giving iodine before blocking synthesis would provide substrate for additional thyroid hormone production.

options: Applying cooling blankets
Administering aspirin for fever reduction
Administering IV propranolol for tachycardia
Administering IV hydrocortisone
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in thyroid storm with a temperature of 105 degrees Fahrenheit. Which intervention should the nurse AVOID?
rationale: Aspirin is contraindicated in thyroid storm because it displaces T4 from binding proteins, increasing free thyroid hormone levels and worsening the crisis. Acetaminophen and cooling blankets should be used instead for fever management in thyroid storm.

options: That PTU was administered at least one hour prior to SSKI, because giving SSKI before PTU provides substrate for more thyroid hormone production
That the patient received aspirin for fever control first
That the patient has eaten a full meal
That radioactive iodine was given simultaneously
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in thyroid storm. The provider orders SSKI. The nurse should verify which critical safety step before administration?
rationale: Before administering SSKI in thyroid storm, the nurse must verify that PTU was given at least one hour prior. Giving SSKI before antithyroid medication provides the overactive thyroid with iodine substrate to produce even more thyroid hormone, which would dangerously worsen the storm.

options: Hyperthermia, tachycardia, and agitation
Hypertension, tremor, and diaphoresis
Hypothermia, bradycardia, altered mental status, and hypotension
Polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss
q: A nurse is assessing a patient with suspected myxedema coma. Which cluster of findings does the nurse expect?
rationale: Myxedema coma is the extreme, life-threatening presentation of hypothyroidism characterized by hypothermia, severe bradycardia, altered mental status progressing to coma, and hypotension. These findings reflect the profound metabolic slowdown caused by critically low thyroid hormone levels.

options: Active rewarming with heated blankets and warm IV fluids
Immersion in warm water to rapidly raise core temperature
Application of heating pads directly to the skin
Passive rewarming only, such as layering blankets, because active rewarming can cause cardiovascular collapse from peripheral vasodilation
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in myxedema coma. The provider orders rewarming measures. Which approach should the nurse use?
rationale: In myxedema coma, only passive rewarming (layering blankets, warm room) should be used because active rewarming causes peripheral vasodilation, which can trigger fatal cardiovascular collapse in a patient with already compromised cardiac output and hypotension from severe hypothyroidism.

options: IV levothyroxine administered slowly and IV hydrocortisone to prevent adrenal crisis
Oral methimazole and oral propranolol
IV SSKI followed by PTU
Oral levothyroxine crushed and given via NG tube only
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in myxedema coma. Which medications does the nurse expect to administer?
rationale: Myxedema coma requires IV levothyroxine administered slowly (to avoid fatal cardiac arrhythmias) and IV hydrocortisone to prevent adrenal crisis. Coexisting adrenal insufficiency is common, and replacing thyroid hormone without cortisol coverage can precipitate acute adrenal failure.

options: aspirin increases the risk of bleeding in hyperthyroid states
aspirin displaces T4 from binding proteins, increasing free thyroid hormone levels and worsening the storm
aspirin directly stimulates the thyroid gland
aspirin interacts with propranolol causing severe hypotension
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in thyroid storm. Why is aspirin contraindicated as an antipyretic in this condition?
rationale: Aspirin is contraindicated in thyroid storm because it displaces T4 from thyroid-binding proteins, increasing the level of free (active) thyroid hormone in circulation. This worsens the hypermetabolic state and can be fatal. Acetaminophen should be used instead.

options: Mild sore throat and difficulty swallowing
Slightly hoarse voice
Tingling and numbness around the mouth and fingertips with a positive Trousseau sign, indicating hypocalcemia
Mild neck swelling at the incision site
q: A nurse is caring for a patient 4 hours post-thyroidectomy. Which assessment finding requires IMMEDIATE intervention?
rationale: Tingling and numbness around the mouth and fingertips with a positive Trousseau sign indicate hypocalcemia from accidental parathyroid gland damage or removal during thyroidectomy. This is a medical emergency because calcium levels below 6-7 mg/dL can cause laryngospasm, seizures, and cardiac arrest.

options: Oral methimazole tablets
Tracheostomy tray and IV calcium gluconate
Nasal cannula and pulse oximeter only
IV potassium chloride
q: A nurse is preparing a post-thyroidectomy patient's bedside. Which item is MOST essential to have immediately available?
rationale: A tracheostomy tray must be at the bedside because post-thyroidectomy hemorrhage or laryngeal edema can cause acute airway obstruction. IV calcium gluconate must also be immediately available to treat hypocalcemia from accidental parathyroid damage, which can cause tetany and laryngospasm.

options: Document the finding and reassess in 30 minutes
Apply an ice pack to the anterior neck
Elevate the head of bed and apply gentle pressure
Notify the surgeon immediately because posterior hemorrhage after thyroidectomy is a surgical emergency that can cause airway compromise
q: A nurse is assessing a patient 6 hours after thyroidectomy and notes a large amount of blood pooling behind the patient's neck when the pillow is lifted. What is the nurse's FIRST action?
rationale: Blood pooling behind the neck after thyroidectomy indicates posterior hemorrhage, which is a surgical emergency because expanding hematoma can compress the trachea and cause airway obstruction. The surgeon must be notified immediately, as the patient may need emergent surgical evacuation.

options: Moon face, buffalo hump, purple striae, truncal obesity, and easy bruising
Bronze skin pigmentation, hypotension, and weight loss
Exophthalmos, tremor, and heat intolerance
Polyuria, polydipsia, and dilute urine
q: A nurse is assessing a patient with Cushing syndrome. Which cluster of findings does the nurse expect?
rationale: Cushing syndrome results from excess cortisol, which causes the classic cluster of moon face, buffalo hump, purple striae, truncal obesity, and easy bruising. These findings reflect cortisol's effects on fat redistribution, protein wasting, collagen breakdown, and capillary fragility.

options: Purple striae and moon face
Hyperpigmented bronze skin, orthostatic hypotension, and unexplained weight loss
Buffalo hump and truncal obesity
Periorbital edema and bradycardia
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Addison disease. Which assessment finding is MOST characteristic of this condition?
rationale: Addison disease causes deficiency of both cortisol and aldosterone. The hyperpigmented bronze skin results from elevated ACTH stimulating melanocytes, while orthostatic hypotension results from aldosterone deficiency causing sodium and water loss, and weight loss reflects chronic cortisol deficiency.

options: Hypoglycemia and salt craving
Weight loss and fatigue
Immunosuppression with poor wound healing and recurrent infections due to cortisol excess
Hypotension and hyperkalemia
q: A nurse is differentiating between Cushing syndrome and Addison disease. Which finding indicates Cushing syndrome rather than Addison disease?
rationale: Immunosuppression with poor wound healing and recurrent infections is a hallmark of Cushing syndrome, where excess cortisol suppresses the immune system. Addison disease (cortisol deficiency) presents with the opposite: hypoglycemia, hypotension, and hyperkalemia rather than immunosuppression.

options: Excess cortisol production causing protein wasting
Excess aldosterone causing potassium wasting
Thyroid hormone excess causing metabolic acceleration
Deficient cortisol and aldosterone causing hypotension, hyponatremia, and inability to mount a stress response
q: A nurse is assessing a patient with Addison disease who reports extreme fatigue, dizziness upon standing, and craving salty foods. The nurse recognizes these findings result from which pathophysiology?
rationale: In Addison disease, deficient cortisol impairs the body's stress response and gluconeogenesis, while deficient aldosterone prevents sodium and water retention, causing hypotension, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia. These combined deficiencies explain the fatigue, dizziness, and salt cravings.

options: Cushing syndrome features thin, fragile skin with purple striae; Addison disease features hyperpigmented bronze skin in creases and sun-exposed areas
Both conditions cause identical skin changes
Cushing syndrome causes bronze pigmentation; Addison disease causes purple striae
Neither condition affects the skin
q: A nurse is comparing skin findings in two patients: one with Cushing syndrome and one with Addison disease. Which assessment finding is correctly matched?
rationale: Cushing syndrome causes thin, fragile skin with purple striae from cortisol-induced protein wasting and collagen breakdown, while Addison disease causes hyperpigmented bronze skin in creases and sun-exposed areas from excess ACTH stimulating melanocyte receptors in response to low cortisol.

options: Decreased cortisol after dexamethasone suppression test
Elevated cortisol that does NOT suppress after dexamethasone administration, with elevated glucose
Elevated ACTH stimulation response
Decreased sodium and elevated potassium
q: A nurse is interpreting laboratory results for a patient with suspected Cushing syndrome. Which finding does the nurse expect?
rationale: In Cushing syndrome, elevated cortisol does NOT suppress after dexamethasone administration because cortisol production is autonomous (from tumor or chronic stimulation). Elevated glucose accompanies this finding because excess cortisol promotes gluconeogenesis and insulin resistance.

options: Dexamethasone suppression test showing unsuppressed cortisol
24-hour urine metanephrines elevated above normal
ACTH stimulation (Cosyntropin) test showing cortisol levels that fail to rise, confirming adrenal insufficiency
Water deprivation test showing concentrated urine
q: A nurse is reviewing diagnostic results for a patient with suspected Addison disease. Which test result confirms the diagnosis?
rationale: The ACTH stimulation (Cosyntropin) test is the definitive diagnostic test for Addison disease. When synthetic ACTH is administered and cortisol levels fail to rise, it confirms the adrenal glands are damaged and unable to respond, confirming adrenal insufficiency.

options: Normal morning cortisol indicating remission
Below the expected range for Cushing syndrome
Indicative of adrenal insufficiency
Significantly elevated above the normal range of 5-23 mcg/dL, consistent with cortisol excess in Cushing syndrome
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Cushing syndrome and reviews the morning cortisol level, which is 42 mcg/dL. The nurse interprets this finding as which of the following?
rationale: A morning cortisol of 42 mcg/dL is significantly elevated above the normal range of 5-23 mcg/dL, consistent with cortisol excess in Cushing syndrome. Morning cortisol is the highest physiologic level; values this elevated indicate pathologic hypercortisolism.

options: Primary adrenal insufficiency where the adrenal glands cannot respond to high ACTH stimulation, confirming Addison disease
Secondary adrenal insufficiency from pituitary failure
Cushing syndrome from an adrenal tumor
Normal physiologic stress response
q: A nurse is reviewing labs for a patient with Addison disease. The cortisol level is 2 mcg/dL and ACTH is markedly elevated. The nurse understands this pattern indicates which condition?
rationale: A cortisol level of 2 mcg/dL (critically low) with markedly elevated ACTH indicates primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease). The pituitary is producing excessive ACTH trying to stimulate the failing adrenal glands, which cannot respond. This pattern distinguishes primary from secondary adrenal insufficiency.

options: Hyponatremia and hyperkalemia
Hypernatremia, hypokalemia, and hyperglycemia
Normal electrolytes with low glucose
Hypercalcemia and hypophosphatemia
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Cushing syndrome. Using the memory aid 'Cushing Keeps Sodium, Kicks out Potassium,' which electrolyte pattern does the nurse expect?
rationale: In Cushing syndrome, excess cortisol acts like aldosterone, causing hypernatremia (sodium retention), hypokalemia (potassium wasting), and hyperglycemia (cortisol promotes gluconeogenesis). The mnemonic 'Cushing Keeps Sodium, Kicks out Potassium' captures this electrolyte pattern.

options: Hypernatremia, hypokalemia, hyperglycemia
Normal sodium with elevated calcium
Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia
Elevated sodium with normal potassium
q: A nurse is assessing a patient with Addison disease. Which electrolyte pattern does the nurse expect?
rationale: In Addison disease, loss of aldosterone means the kidneys cannot retain sodium or excrete potassium, resulting in hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia. This is the opposite of Cushing syndrome and reflects the combined deficiency of both cortisol and aldosterone.

options: Atrial fibrillation
Prolonged QT interval
Sinus tachycardia
Tall, peaked T waves progressing to ventricular fibrillation, as hyperkalemia is a life-threatening arrhythmia risk
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Addison disease whose potassium is 6.2 mEq/L. Which cardiac complication should the nurse monitor for FIRST?
rationale: Potassium of 6.2 mEq/L is dangerously elevated and can cause tall, peaked T waves progressing to ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest. Hyperkalemia from Addison disease is life-threatening because aldosterone deficiency prevents normal renal potassium excretion, requiring immediate cardiac monitoring and intervention.

options: Excess cortisol promotes gluconeogenesis and insulin resistance, causing persistent hyperglycemia
Decreased cortisol impairs glucose metabolism
Excess aldosterone directly raises blood glucose
Cortisol deficiency triggers glycogenolysis
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Cushing syndrome whose glucose is 312 mg/dL. The nurse understands the hyperglycemia results from which pathophysiologic mechanism?
rationale: Excess cortisol in Cushing syndrome promotes gluconeogenesis (production of new glucose in the liver) and causes insulin resistance in peripheral tissues, resulting in persistent hyperglycemia sometimes called 'steroid diabetes.' This mechanism explains the glucose of 312 mg/dL.

options: Take the full daily dose at bedtime to mimic natural cortisol rhythm
Take two-thirds of the dose in the morning and one-third in the afternoon to mimic the diurnal cortisol pattern, and NEVER stop the medication abruptly
Take it only on days when feeling fatigued
Crush and mix with grapefruit juice for better absorption
q: A nurse is teaching a patient with Addison disease about hydrocortisone replacement. Which instruction is MOST important?
rationale: Hydrocortisone replacement in Addison disease should follow the body's natural diurnal cortisol pattern: two-thirds of the dose in the morning (when cortisol peaks) and one-third in the afternoon. The medication must NEVER be stopped abruptly, as this can precipitate life-threatening Addisonian crisis.

options: It replaces thyroid hormone lost from adrenal failure
It blocks excess cortisol production
fludrocortisone replaces aldosterone, helping your body retain sodium and water to maintain blood pressure while excreting excess potassium
It stimulates the adrenal glands to produce more cortisol
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Addison disease who also takes fludrocortisone. The patient asks what this medication does. Which response is BEST?
rationale: Fludrocortisone is a synthetic mineralocorticoid that replaces aldosterone in Addison disease. It helps the kidneys retain sodium and water to maintain blood pressure while promoting excretion of excess potassium, correcting the dangerous electrolyte imbalances caused by aldosterone deficiency.

options: Stimulating ACTH to suppress the tumor
Replacing deficient cortisol levels
Blocking aldosterone receptors in the kidney
Inhibiting adrenal cortisol synthesis to reduce hypercortisolism before surgery
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Cushing syndrome caused by an adrenal adenoma. The patient takes ketoconazole preoperatively. The nurse understands this medication works by which mechanism?
rationale: Ketoconazole works by inhibiting adrenal cortisol synthesis, reducing hypercortisolism in Cushing syndrome before surgical removal of the adrenal adenoma. This preoperative management helps normalize cortisol levels to reduce surgical complications.

options: I need to double or triple my hydrocortisone dose during illness, surgery, or significant physical stress, and carry injectable hydrocortisone for emergencies
I should stop my hydrocortisone when I get sick to avoid immunosuppression
I only need to increase my dose if I have a fever above 104 degrees
Stress dosing is only necessary during hospitalization
q: A nurse is teaching a patient with Addison disease about stress dosing of hydrocortisone. Which patient statement indicates understanding?
rationale: Patients with Addison disease must double or triple their hydrocortisone dose during illness, surgery, or significant physical stress because their adrenal glands cannot mount an appropriate cortisol stress response. Carrying an injectable hydrocortisone emergency kit is essential for situations where oral medication cannot be taken.

options: Fluid restriction and demeclocycline administration
IV hydrocortisone 100 mg STAT, IV normal saline with D5 for volume and glucose replacement, and continuous vital sign monitoring
Oral fludrocortisone and low-sodium diet
IV furosemide and potassium replacement
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in Addisonian crisis. Which interventions does the nurse anticipate? Select the BEST answer.
rationale: Addisonian crisis requires IV hydrocortisone 100 mg STAT for immediate cortisol replacement, IV normal saline with D5 for volume resuscitation and glucose correction, and continuous vital sign monitoring. This combination addresses the circulatory collapse, hypoglycemia, and cortisol deficiency simultaneously.

options: Thyroid storm
Myxedema coma
Addisonian crisis precipitated by physiologic stress in a patient with insufficient cortisol reserve
SIADH
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with known Addison disease who presents with severe hypotension (BP 72/40), confusion, and a glucose of 48 mg/dL. The nurse recognizes this is MOST likely which emergency?
rationale: Severe hypotension (BP 72/40), confusion, and glucose of 48 mg/dL in a patient with known Addison disease represent Addisonian crisis precipitated by physiologic stress. Without adequate cortisol reserve, the body cannot maintain blood pressure, blood glucose, or mount an appropriate stress response, leading to circulatory collapse.

options: Administer oral potassium supplements
Restrict dietary potassium intake
Administer a thiazide diuretic
Administer IV calcium gluconate for cardiac protection, followed by insulin and glucose to shift potassium intracellularly
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in Addisonian crisis with a potassium of 6.8 mEq/L. Which intervention should the nurse anticipate FIRST?
rationale: With a potassium of 6.8 mEq/L, the immediate priority is cardiac protection with IV calcium gluconate, followed by insulin and glucose to shift potassium intracellularly. Severe hyperkalemia causes fatal cardiac arrhythmias, making cardiac stabilization the first intervention before addressing the underlying cause.

options: IV hydrocortisone provides immediate cortisol replacement in a hemodynamically unstable patient who may not be able to absorb oral medications due to hypotension and altered perfusion
Oral hydrocortisone would cause nausea in Addisonian crisis
IV hydrocortisone is less expensive than oral formulations
Oral hydrocortisone is contraindicated in all adrenal disorders
q: A nurse is caring for a patient in Addisonian crisis. The provider orders IV hydrocortisone. Why is IV rather than oral administration essential in this situation?
rationale: IV hydrocortisone is essential in Addisonian crisis because the patient is hemodynamically unstable with hypotension and altered perfusion, which impairs oral medication absorption. The IV route provides immediate, reliable cortisol replacement to a patient who may also have nausea, vomiting, or altered consciousness.

options: I should wear a medical alert bracelet at all times
I will carry an emergency injection kit of hydrocortisone
I can safely skip my hydrocortisone for a few days if I feel well, since the medication is only needed during illness
I will notify my provider before any dental or surgical procedures
q: A nurse is teaching a patient with Addison disease about preventing Addisonian crisis. Which statement by the patient requires additional teaching?
rationale: Stating one can 'safely skip hydrocortisone for a few days if feeling well' demonstrates a dangerous misunderstanding that requires additional teaching. Hydrocortisone replaces essential cortisol the body cannot produce; abruptly stopping it can precipitate life-threatening Addisonian crisis regardless of how the patient feels.

options: Avoid all physical activity to prevent adrenal stress
Limit fluid intake to prevent edema
Taper and discontinue hydrocortisone once symptoms resolve
Never abruptly discontinue corticosteroids, as sudden withdrawal can precipitate life-threatening Addisonian crisis
q: A nurse is discharging a patient with Addison disease. Which safety instruction is MOST critical?
rationale: The most critical safety instruction is to never abruptly discontinue corticosteroids, as sudden withdrawal in a patient with suppressed adrenal function can precipitate life-threatening Addisonian crisis. The HPA axis requires gradual tapering to resume endogenous cortisol production.

options: Ensuring perioperative corticosteroid coverage because the remaining adrenal gland may be suppressed, risking acute adrenal insufficiency after tumor removal
Administering radioactive iodine preoperatively
Starting the patient on PTU before surgery
Discontinuing all antihypertensives 48 hours before surgery
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Cushing syndrome scheduled for adrenalectomy. Which preoperative safety consideration is MOST important?
rationale: After adrenalectomy for Cushing syndrome, the remaining adrenal gland may be suppressed from chronic cortisol excess and unable to respond to surgical stress. Perioperative corticosteroid coverage is essential to prevent acute adrenal insufficiency, which can cause hemodynamic collapse during or after surgery.

options: Apply a moisturizing lotion to prevent skin breakdown
Administer insulin as prescribed and implement strict aseptic wound care, as cortisol excess causes immunosuppression and impaired healing
Encourage high-calorie supplements
Ambulate the patient to promote circulation
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with Cushing syndrome who has a blood glucose of 342 mg/dL and an open wound with purulent drainage. Which nursing priority should be addressed FIRST?
rationale: The priority in a Cushing patient with hyperglycemia and an infected wound is to administer insulin for glucose control and implement strict aseptic wound care. Excess cortisol causes both immunosuppression (increasing infection risk) and impaired wound healing, making infection management critical.

options: Both conditions cause hypernatremia
Both conditions result in concentrated urine
SIADH causes water retention leading to dilutional hyponatremia with concentrated urine, while DI causes water loss leading to hypernatremia with dilute urine
DI causes fluid overload while SIADH causes dehydration
q: A nurse is comparing SIADH and diabetes insipidus. Which statement accurately differentiates the two conditions?
rationale: SIADH causes excessive water retention leading to dilutional hyponatremia with concentrated urine, while diabetes insipidus causes massive water loss leading to hypernatremia with dilute urine. These conditions are physiologic opposites: SIADH retains too much water, and DI loses too much water.

options: Polyuria of 8 liters per day and extreme thirst
Hypernatremia and dry mucous membranes
Elevated serum osmolality and dilute urine
Decreased sodium, decreased serum osmolality, concentrated urine, and decreased urine output
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with SIADH. Which assessment findings does the nurse expect?
rationale: In SIADH, excess ADH causes the kidneys to retain water, resulting in decreased sodium, decreased serum osmolality (dilute serum), concentrated urine (high osmolality), and decreased urine output. The hyponatremia is dilutional, caused by water excess rather than true sodium deficit.

options: Urine specific gravity less than 1.005, elevated sodium, elevated serum osmolality, and intense thirst
Concentrated urine with high specific gravity
Hyponatremia with fluid overload
Decreased serum osmolality with weight gain
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with diabetes insipidus who has a urine output of 800 mL in the past hour. Which additional finding does the nurse expect?
rationale: Diabetes insipidus presents with massive polyuria and dilute urine (urine specific gravity less than 1.005) due to inadequate ADH action. The resulting free water loss causes elevated sodium, elevated serum osmolality, and intense thirst as the body attempts to compensate for dehydration.

options: SIADH
Diabetes insipidus characterized by massive polyuria, extremely dilute urine, and hypernatremia from free water loss
Addisonian crisis
Cushing syndrome
q: A nurse is assessing a patient and notes urine output of 15 liters in 24 hours, urine specific gravity of 1.001, and sodium of 158 mEq/L. This clinical picture is MOST consistent with which condition?
rationale: Urine output of 15 liters in 24 hours with extremely dilute urine (urine specific gravity 1.001) and sodium of 158 mEq/L is the classic presentation of diabetes insipidus. The massive free water loss through the kidneys concentrates the serum, causing dangerous hypernatremia and dehydration.

options: Administer oral sodium chloride tablets
Restrict fluids to 800 mL per day
Administer demeclocycline as a long-term management strategy
Administer hypertonic saline (3% NaCl) via IV pump with frequent sodium monitoring, correcting no faster than 8-12 mEq per 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with SIADH whose sodium is 118 mEq/L. The patient is having seizures. Which intervention is the PRIORITY?
rationale: With a sodium of 118 mEq/L and active seizures, the priority is administering hypertonic saline (3% NaCl) via IV pump with frequent monitoring. Correction must not exceed 8-12 mEq per 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome, a devastating and irreversible neurological complication.

options: Sodium 122 mEq/L, serum osmolality 258 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 550 mOsm/kg
Sodium 155 mEq/L, serum osmolality 310 mOsm/kg, dilute urine
Normal sodium with elevated potassium
Elevated calcium with decreased phosphorus
q: A nurse is reviewing labs for a patient with suspected SIADH. Which lab pattern is consistent with this diagnosis?
rationale: SIADH presents with sodium of 122 mEq/L (hyponatremia), serum osmolality of 258 mOsm/kg (low, dilute serum), and urine osmolality of 550 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated). This pattern confirms the kidneys are retaining water despite already-dilute serum, which is the hallmark of excess ADH.

options: Low sodium, low serum osmolality, concentrated urine
Elevated sodium, elevated serum osmolality, urine specific gravity less than 1.005, and low urine osmolality
Normal sodium with elevated glucose
Hypokalemia with metabolic alkalosis
q: A nurse is reviewing labs for a patient with diabetes insipidus. Which values does the nurse expect?
rationale: Diabetes insipidus presents with elevated sodium, elevated serum osmolality, and dilute urine with urine specific gravity less than 1.005 and low urine osmolality. The kidneys cannot concentrate urine due to insufficient ADH action, causing massive free water loss and serum concentration.

options: Hyperkalemia leading to cardiac arrest
Hypercalcemia causing kidney stones
Osmotic demyelination syndrome from correcting sodium too rapidly, as the correction exceeds the safe rate of 8-12 mEq per 24 hours
Thyroid storm from electrolyte imbalance
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with SIADH whose sodium was 119 mEq/L six hours ago and is now 126 mEq/L. The nurse should be MOST concerned about which complication?
rationale: A sodium rise from 119 to 126 mEq/L in 6 hours (7 mEq increase) approaches the safe correction limit of 8-12 mEq per 24 hours. Correcting sodium too rapidly in SIADH causes osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis), which results in irreversible neurological damage.

options: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
SIADH confirmed
Primary polydipsia
Central diabetes insipidus, because the kidneys respond to exogenous ADH (DDAVP), confirming the defect is in ADH production, not kidney response
q: A nurse is interpreting a water deprivation test for a patient with suspected diabetes insipidus. After DDAVP administration, urine osmolality increases significantly. What does this result indicate?
rationale: When DDAVP administration during a water deprivation test causes urine osmolality to increase significantly, this confirms central diabetes insipidus. The kidneys respond normally to exogenous ADH, proving the defect is in pituitary ADH production rather than kidney resistance.

options: Central diabetes insipidus responding to ADH replacement
SIADH with excess ADH production
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, because the kidneys do not respond to exogenous ADH
Normal pituitary function
q: A nurse is reviewing a water deprivation test where DDAVP administration does NOT increase urine osmolality. The nurse understands this result indicates which condition?
rationale: When DDAVP does NOT increase urine osmolality, this confirms nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. The kidneys do not respond to exogenous ADH, indicating the defect is at the renal level. Common causes include lithium use and hypercalcemia.

options: DDAVP replaces the ADH my body cannot make, which will help my kidneys reabsorb water and reduce my excessive urination
DDAVP will stimulate my pituitary gland to produce more ADH
I should take DDAVP only when I feel thirsty
DDAVP blocks ADH to help me excrete more water
q: A nurse is teaching a patient with central diabetes insipidus about desmopressin (DDAVP). Which statement by the patient indicates correct understanding?
rationale: DDAVP (desmopressin) is a synthetic form of ADH that replaces the hormone the body cannot produce in central diabetes insipidus. It acts on the renal collecting ducts to promote water reabsorption, reducing the excessive urination and preventing dangerous dehydration and hypernatremia.

options: DDAVP nasal spray
A thiazide diuretic, which paradoxically reduces urine output in nephrogenic DI by promoting sodium and water reabsorption in the proximal tubule
demeclocycline to block ADH
hypertonic saline infusion
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. Which medication does the nurse expect to be prescribed?
rationale: In nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, the kidneys do not respond to ADH, so DDAVP is ineffective. A thiazide diuretic is used because it paradoxically reduces urine output by promoting sodium and water reabsorption in the proximal tubule, decreasing the volume of fluid reaching the collecting ducts.

options: DDAVP to increase water reabsorption
fludrocortisone to retain sodium
demeclocycline, which blocks ADH action at the renal collecting ducts to promote free water excretion
hydrocortisone for cortisol replacement
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with SIADH. Which medication is used for long-term management to promote free water excretion?
rationale: Demeclocycline is used for long-term management of SIADH because it blocks ADH action at the renal collecting ducts, promoting excretion of free water without sodium loss. This helps correct the dilutional hyponatremia caused by excessive ADH-mediated water retention.

options: Infuse the hypertonic saline rapidly to correct the sodium deficit
Administer via peripheral IV without monitoring
Give as an IV bolus over 5 minutes
Administer via IV pump with frequent sodium level monitoring every 2-4 hours, ensuring correction does not exceed 8-12 mEq in 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with SIADH and severe hyponatremia. The provider orders 3% NaCl. Which nursing action is MOST critical during administration?
rationale: When administering 3% NaCl for severe SIADH hyponatremia, the nurse must use an IV pump with frequent sodium monitoring every 2-4 hours and ensure correction does not exceed 8-12 mEq in 24 hours. Rapid correction causes osmotic demyelination syndrome, a devastating neurological complication.

options: Implement fluid restriction as the first-line treatment for mild to moderate SIADH
Administer hypertonic saline immediately
Prepare for emergent dialysis
Administer IV furosemide
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with SIADH whose sodium is 128 mEq/L and who is asymptomatic. What is the PRIORITY intervention?
rationale: For asymptomatic SIADH with sodium of 128 mEq/L, fluid restriction (typically 500-1000 mL/day) is the first-line treatment. This reduces water intake to allow the kidneys to gradually excrete excess free water and correct the dilutional hyponatremia. Hypertonic saline is reserved for symptomatic or severe cases.

options: Restrict oral fluids to prevent fluid overload
Ensure adequate fluid replacement to match output and prevent hypovolemic shock, while monitoring sodium and serum osmolality closely
Administer demeclocycline to reduce urine output
Prepare for immediate surgery
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with diabetes insipidus who has a urine output of 1200 mL in the past 2 hours. What is the nurse's PRIORITY action?
rationale: With a urine output of 1200 mL in 2 hours, the priority in diabetes insipidus is ensuring adequate fluid replacement to match output and prevent hypovolemic shock. The nurse must closely monitor sodium and serum osmolality while replacing free water losses to prevent dangerous dehydration and hypernatremia.

options: SIADH from cerebral edema
Addisonian crisis
Central diabetes insipidus from damage to the posterior pituitary or hypothalamus during surgery
Cushing syndrome from surgical stress
q: A nurse is caring for a post-craniotomy patient who develops sudden polyuria of 500 mL per hour with dilute urine. The nurse suspects which complication?
rationale: Sudden polyuria with dilute urine after craniotomy strongly suggests central diabetes insipidus from damage to the posterior pituitary or hypothalamus during surgery. The posterior pituitary stores and releases ADH; surgical disruption causes deficient ADH release and massive renal free water loss.

options: Stimulating ADH release from the posterior pituitary
Blocking sodium reabsorption in the proximal tubule
Replacing deficient ADH in the collecting ducts
Blocking vasopressin (ADH) receptors in the kidney, causing excretion of free water without sodium loss (aquaresis)
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with SIADH and reviews the provider's order for tolvaptan. The nurse understands this medication works by which mechanism?
rationale: Tolvaptan is a vasopressin (ADH) receptor antagonist that works by blocking ADH receptors in the kidney, causing excretion of free water without sodium loss (aquaresis). This selectively removes excess water retained by SIADH while preserving electrolyte balance.

options: Drink extra fluids the night before the test to ensure adequate hydration
Continue all medications including DDAVP during the test
The test requires withholding fluids while monitoring body weight, urine osmolality, and serum osmolality to determine if ADH is being produced and whether the kidneys respond
The test involves drinking large amounts of water and measuring absorption
q: A nurse is preparing a patient for a water deprivation test. Which instruction should the nurse provide?
rationale: The water deprivation test requires withholding fluids while monitoring body weight, urine osmolality, and serum osmolality. This determines whether the patient can concentrate urine when dehydrated and whether the kidneys respond to exogenous DDAVP, differentiating central from nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.

options: Central diabetes insipidus, because the kidneys can concentrate urine when given exogenous ADH, proving the defect is in ADH production
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
SIADH
Primary polydipsia
q: A nurse is reviewing the results of a water deprivation test. Urine remains dilute despite fluid restriction, but concentrates after DDAVP administration. Which condition is confirmed?
rationale: When urine remains dilute during fluid restriction but concentrates after DDAVP administration, this confirms central diabetes insipidus. The kidneys can concentrate urine when given exogenous ADH, proving the defect is in ADH production by the pituitary, not in kidney responsiveness.

options: Tetany, positive Chvostek sign, and muscle spasms
Pathologic fractures, kidney stones, abdominal pain, and fatigue or depression from hypercalcemia
Seizures, prolonged QT interval, and laryngospasm
Numbness, tingling, and carpopedal spasm
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with hyperparathyroidism. Which assessment findings does the nurse expect based on the mnemonic 'Bones, Stones, Groans, Moans'?
rationale: Hyperparathyroidism causes excess PTH to mobilize calcium from bones, resulting in the classic 'Bones, Stones, Groans, Moans' presentation: pathologic fractures and bone pain (Bones), calcium kidney stones (Stones), abdominal pain and constipation (Groans), and fatigue or depression (Moans) from hypercalcemia.

options: Kidney stones and bone pain
Abdominal pain and constipation
Seizures, prolonged QT interval, muscle tetany, carpopedal spasm, and perioral numbness from hypocalcemia
Polyuria and flank pain
q: A nurse is assessing a patient with hypoparathyroidism. Which findings does the nurse expect based on the mnemonic 'CATS go numb' — Convulsions, Arrhythmias, Tetany, Spasms?
rationale: Hypoparathyroidism causes hypocalcemia, which produces the classic 'CATS go numb' findings: Convulsions (seizures), Arrhythmias (prolonged QT interval), Tetany (carpopedal spasm), and Spasms with perioral numbness. These reflect increased neuromuscular excitability from low calcium levels.

options: Prolonged QT interval
Tall peaked T waves
ST segment elevation
Shortened QT interval, as elevated calcium accelerates cardiac repolarization
q: A nurse is differentiating between hyperparathyroidism and hypoparathyroidism. Which ECG change is associated with hypercalcemia in hyperparathyroidism?
rationale: Hypercalcemia in hyperparathyroidism causes a shortened QT interval because elevated calcium accelerates cardiac repolarization. This is the opposite of hypocalcemia (hypoparathyroidism), which prolongs the QT interval. Both ECG changes can lead to dangerous arrhythmias at extreme levels.

options: Assess for Trousseau sign (carpal spasm with BP cuff inflation) and Chvostek sign (facial twitch on tapping facial nerve), which indicate hypocalcemia-induced neuromuscular excitability
Assess for Kernig sign and Brudzinski sign
Perform a Romberg test
Check patellar deep tendon reflexes only
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with post-surgical hypoparathyroidism after thyroidectomy. The calcium level is 6.8 mg/dL. Which assessment technique should the nurse perform to evaluate for neuromuscular irritability?
rationale: With a calcium of 6.8 mg/dL (critically low), the nurse should assess for Trousseau sign (carpal spasm when a BP cuff is inflated on the arm) and Chvostek sign (facial twitch when the facial nerve is tapped). Both signs indicate hypocalcemia-induced neuromuscular excitability that can progress to tetany and seizures.

options: Decreased calcium, elevated phosphorus, decreased PTH
Elevated PTH, elevated calcium, decreased phosphorus
Normal calcium with elevated TSH
Decreased PTH with elevated calcium
q: A nurse is reviewing labs for a patient with primary hyperparathyroidism. Which lab pattern does the nurse expect?
rationale: Primary hyperparathyroidism presents with elevated PTH, elevated calcium, and decreased phosphorus. Excess PTH pulls calcium from bones and increases renal calcium reabsorption while promoting phosphorus excretion, creating the characteristic inverse calcium-phosphorus relationship.

options: Elevated calcium and decreased phosphorus
Normal calcium and phosphorus
Decreased PTH, decreased calcium, and elevated phosphorus
Elevated PTH with normal calcium
q: A nurse is reviewing labs for a patient with hypoparathyroidism. Which pattern is expected?
rationale: Hypoparathyroidism presents with decreased PTH, decreased calcium, and elevated phosphorus. Without adequate PTH, the body cannot mobilize calcium from bones or promote renal phosphorus excretion, resulting in hypocalcemia and hyperphosphatemia.

options: Hypoparathyroidism
Addison disease
SIADH
Hyperparathyroidism, where excess PTH raises calcium and lowers phosphorus through increased renal phosphorus excretion
q: A nurse is caring for a patient whose calcium is 12.8 mg/dL and phosphorus is 1.9 mg/dL. The nurse recognizes this inverse relationship between calcium and phosphorus is characteristic of which condition?
rationale: A calcium of 12.8 mg/dL with phosphorus of 1.9 mg/dL demonstrates the classic inverse calcium-phosphorus relationship of hyperparathyroidism. Excess PTH raises calcium by mobilizing it from bones and increases renal phosphorus excretion, lowering phosphorus levels.

options: A positive Trousseau sign, which indicates hypocalcemia-induced neuromuscular irritability
A positive Chvostek sign
A positive Homans sign
A positive Babinski sign
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with a calcium level of 6.5 mg/dL. The nurse inflates a blood pressure cuff on the patient's arm and observes carpal spasm after 3 minutes. The nurse documents this as which finding?
rationale: A positive Trousseau sign is carpal spasm occurring after inflating a blood pressure cuff for 3 minutes, indicating hypocalcemia-induced neuromuscular irritability. With a calcium of 6.5 mg/dL (critically low), this sign confirms the patient is at risk for tetany, seizures, and laryngospasm.

options: Positive Trousseau sign
Positive Chvostek sign indicating hypocalcemia-related neuromuscular excitability
Positive Kernig sign
Positive Romberg sign
q: A nurse taps the facial nerve anterior to the ear in a patient with suspected hypocalcemia and observes ipsilateral facial muscle twitching. Which finding does the nurse document?
rationale: Tapping the facial nerve anterior to the ear and observing ipsilateral facial muscle twitching is a positive Chvostek sign, indicating hypocalcemia-related neuromuscular excitability. This bedside test, along with Trousseau sign, helps quickly identify dangerous hypocalcemia requiring prompt calcium replacement.

options: Hypocalcemia causing muscle spasms that radiate to the bones
Excess ADH causing fluid retention and kidney pressure
Excess PTH mobilizes calcium from bones causing osteoporosis and fractures, while hypercalcemia leads to calcium kidney stone formation
Cortisol excess weakening bones and kidneys
q: A nurse is assessing a patient with hyperparathyroidism who reports bone pain and flank pain. The nurse understands these symptoms result from which pathophysiology?
rationale: In hyperparathyroidism, excess PTH mobilizes calcium from bones, causing osteoporosis and pathologic fractures (bone pain). The resulting hypercalcemia leads to calcium precipitation in the renal tubules, forming kidney stones (flank pain). This is the pathophysiology behind 'Bones and Stones.'

options: Fluid restriction and thiazide diuretics
Oral calcium supplements and vitamin D
IV dextrose and insulin
IV normal saline for aggressive hydration, IV furosemide to promote calcium excretion, calcitonin for rapid calcium reduction, and bisphosphonates for sustained effect
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with severe hypercalcemia from hyperparathyroidism (calcium 14.2 mg/dL). Which treatment does the nurse anticipate?
rationale: Severe hypercalcemia (calcium 14.2 mg/dL) from hyperparathyroidism requires aggressive treatment: IV normal saline for hydration and dilution, IV furosemide to promote renal calcium excretion, calcitonin for rapid but temporary calcium reduction, and bisphosphonates for sustained effect by inhibiting bone resorption.

options: Administer IV calcium gluconate slowly with continuous cardiac monitoring, as rapid administration can cause cardiac arrest
Administer oral calcium carbonate tablets
Apply warm compresses to affected muscles
Encourage weight-bearing exercise
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with acute symptomatic hypocalcemia (calcium 5.9 mg/dL) with tetany. Which intervention is the PRIORITY?
rationale: Acute symptomatic hypocalcemia with tetany requires immediate IV calcium gluconate administered slowly with continuous cardiac monitoring. Rapid administration of IV calcium can cause cardiac arrest. This is the priority intervention to reverse dangerous neuromuscular excitability and prevent seizures and laryngospasm.

options: methimazole and propranolol for thyroid control
Oral calcium supplements and calcitriol (active vitamin D) to maintain adequate calcium levels, with regular monitoring of calcium and phosphorus
furosemide and IV normal saline for calcium excretion
bisphosphonates and calcitonin to lower calcium
q: A nurse is teaching a patient with chronic hypoparathyroidism about long-term management. Which medications should the nurse discuss?
rationale: Chronic hypoparathyroidism requires lifelong oral calcium supplements and calcitriol (active vitamin D) to maintain adequate calcium levels. Calcitriol is necessary because without PTH, the kidneys cannot activate vitamin D, which is essential for intestinal calcium absorption.

options: Seizure precautions and padded side rails
Strict intake and output with daily weights
Encouraging ambulation and weight-bearing activity to reduce bone demineralization, maintaining adequate hydration, and monitoring for cardiac dysrhythmias
Applying sequential compression devices
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with hypercalcemia. Which safety precaution is MOST important?
rationale: For patients with hypercalcemia, the most important safety precautions include encouraging ambulation and weight-bearing activity to reduce further bone demineralization, maintaining adequate hydration to prevent kidney stone formation, and monitoring for cardiac dysrhythmias (shortened QT, heart blocks) from elevated calcium.

options: Encourage ambulation to promote calcium absorption
Restrict fluid intake to concentrate serum calcium
Provide a high-phosphorus diet
Implement seizure precautions, keep IV calcium gluconate at bedside, maintain a quiet environment to minimize stimulation that could trigger tetany or seizures
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with severe hypocalcemia. Which safety measure should the nurse implement?
rationale: Severe hypocalcemia requires seizure precautions, keeping IV calcium gluconate at the bedside for emergency replacement, and maintaining a quiet environment to minimize stimulation. Excessive stimulation such as bright lights, loud noises, or sudden movements can trigger tetany, laryngospasm, or seizures in hypocalcemic patients.

options: Pressure (severe hypertension), Pain (headache), Perspiration (diaphoresis), Palpitations, and Pallor
Polyuria, Polydipsia, Polyphagia, Paresthesia, and Pruritus
Petechiae, Purpura, Pain, Pruritus, and Pallor
Ptosis, Pupil constriction, Paresthesia, Paralysis, and Photophobia
q: A nurse is assessing a patient with suspected pheochromocytoma. Which cluster of symptoms known as the '5 Ps' does the nurse expect?
rationale: The '5 Ps' of pheochromocytoma are: Pressure (severe paroxysmal hypertension), Pain (severe headache), Perspiration (profuse diaphoresis), Palpitations (tachycardia), and Pallor (vasoconstriction). These result from massive catecholamine surges released by the adrenal medulla tumor.

options: Excess cortisol from the adrenal cortex
Catecholamine surges (epinephrine and norepinephrine) released by the adrenal medulla tumor causing intense sympathetic activation
Aldosterone excess causing fluid retention
Excess thyroid hormone release
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with pheochromocytoma who experiences episodic severe headaches, profuse sweating, and blood pressure spikes to 240/130 mmHg. The nurse attributes these episodes to which pathophysiology?
rationale: Pheochromocytoma episodes are caused by catecholamine surges (epinephrine and norepinephrine) released from the adrenal medulla tumor, producing intense sympathetic activation. This explains the severe hypertension (BP 240/130), profuse sweating, and pounding headaches during paroxysmal episodes.

options: Dexamethasone suppression test
ACTH stimulation test
24-hour urine collection for metanephrines and VMA, which measures catecholamine metabolites produced by the tumor
Water deprivation test
q: A nurse is preparing a patient with suspected pheochromocytoma for diagnostic testing. Which test is the gold standard for diagnosis?
rationale: The 24-hour urine collection for metanephrines and VMA (vanillylmandelic acid) is the gold standard diagnostic test for pheochromocytoma. These are catecholamine metabolites produced by the tumor, and their elevation in urine confirms excessive catecholamine production.

options: Excess cortisol production
Thyroid hormone overproduction
Aldosterone-secreting tumor
Excessive catecholamine production from a chromaffin cell tumor of the adrenal medulla
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with pheochromocytoma whose 24-hour urine metanephrines and VMA are markedly elevated. The nurse understands these results confirm which finding?
rationale: Markedly elevated 24-hour urine metanephrines and VMA confirm excessive catecholamine production from a chromaffin cell tumor of the adrenal medulla. Pheochromocytoma produces massive amounts of epinephrine and norepinephrine, which are metabolized to metanephrines and VMA and excreted in urine.

options: phenoxybenzamine (alpha-adrenergic blocker) must be started FIRST to control hypertension before any beta-blocker is introduced
propranolol (beta-blocker) should be started first
methimazole to control metabolic rate
hydrocortisone for stress coverage only
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with pheochromocytoma scheduled for adrenalectomy. Which medication must be started FIRST before surgery?
rationale: Phenoxybenzamine, an alpha-adrenergic blocker, must be started FIRST (10-14 days preoperatively) to control the severe hypertension caused by catecholamine excess. Beta-blockers are added only AFTER alpha blockade is established to prevent unopposed alpha stimulation, which would cause a hypertensive crisis.

options: That the patient has eaten a full meal
That phenoxybenzamine (alpha blockade) was established FIRST, because giving a beta-blocker without alpha blockade can cause unopposed alpha stimulation leading to a hypertensive crisis
That the patient's TSH is within normal limits
That the patient's calcium level is above 8.5 mg/dL
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with pheochromocytoma. The provider orders propranolol. Before administering this medication, the nurse should verify which critical safety measure?
rationale: Before administering propranolol (beta-blocker), the nurse must verify that phenoxybenzamine (alpha blockade) was established FIRST. Giving a beta-blocker without alpha blockade removes beta-mediated vasodilation, allowing unopposed alpha-receptor stimulation that can trigger a lethal hypertensive crisis.

options: To suppress catecholamine production entirely
To block TSH receptors on the adrenal gland
To prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis from catecholamine surges during tumor manipulation by establishing adequate alpha-adrenergic blockade
To increase blood pressure stability through beta-receptor stimulation
q: A nurse is preparing a patient with pheochromocytoma for surgery. The surgeon explains the importance of alpha blockade with phenoxybenzamine for 10-14 days preoperatively. The nurse understands the rationale is which of the following?
rationale: Alpha-adrenergic blockade with phenoxybenzamine for 10-14 days preoperatively is essential to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis during tumor manipulation. Without adequate alpha blockade, surgical handling of the pheochromocytoma can trigger massive catecholamine release causing life-threatening blood pressure spikes.

options: Measuring blood pressure in both arms
Auscultating heart sounds
Drawing blood for catecholamine levels
Palpating the abdomen, which can compress the tumor and cause a massive catecholamine release
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with a suspected pheochromocytoma. Which action by the nurse could trigger a life-threatening hypertensive crisis?
rationale: Palpating the abdomen of a patient with suspected pheochromocytoma can compress the tumor and trigger a massive catecholamine release, causing a life-threatening hypertensive crisis. The nurse must avoid abdominal palpation and minimize other triggers such as Valsalva maneuver and emotional stress.

options: Administer IV phentolamine or nitroprusside as prescribed for immediate blood pressure reduction while avoiding abdominal palpation
Elevate the head of bed and apply a cold cloth
Administer oral propranolol and reassess in 1 hour
Prepare for immediate thyroidectomy
q: A nurse is caring for a patient with pheochromocytoma who is experiencing a hypertensive crisis with a blood pressure of 260/140 mmHg. Which action is the PRIORITY?
rationale: A hypertensive crisis (BP 260/140) in pheochromocytoma requires immediate IV phentolamine (alpha-blocker) or nitroprusside for rapid blood pressure reduction. Abdominal palpation must be strictly avoided to prevent further catecholamine surges. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate pharmacologic intervention.

options: Encourage vigorous exercise to reduce catecholamine levels
Avoid palpating the abdomen, minimize stress, monitor blood pressure frequently, and keep phentolamine available for hypertensive emergencies
Restrict all fluids to prevent hypertension
Apply abdominal binders to stabilize the tumor
q: A nurse is developing a care plan for a patient with pheochromocytoma awaiting surgery. Which nursing intervention is essential for safety?
rationale: Key nursing interventions for pheochromocytoma include avoiding abdominal palpation (which can trigger catecholamine release), minimizing stress, monitoring blood pressure frequently, and keeping phentolamine available for hypertensive emergencies. These precautions prevent life-threatening blood pressure crises before definitive surgical treatment.

options: Cushing syndrome
Graves disease
Addison disease, which presents with cortisol and aldosterone deficiency causing characteristic electrolyte disturbances and hyperpigmentation
Hyperparathyroidism
q: A nurse is caring for a patient who presents with hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and bronze skin. These findings are consistent with which endocrine disorder?
rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and bronze skin are the classic findings of Addison disease. The electrolyte imbalances result from aldosterone deficiency (cannot retain sodium or excrete potassium), while the bronze hyperpigmentation results from elevated ACTH stimulating melanocytes.

options: A patient with hypothyroidism who reports constipation
A patient with Cushing syndrome who has a glucose of 210 mg/dL
A patient with SIADH whose sodium is 131 mEq/L and is asymptomatic
A patient with Addison disease who has a blood pressure of 76/42 mmHg, is confused, and has a glucose of 42 mg/dL, suggesting Addisonian crisis
q: A nurse is caring for multiple patients on an endocrine unit. Which patient should the nurse assess FIRST?
rationale: The patient with Addison disease in crisis (BP 76/42, confused, glucose 42 mg/dL) requires the most immediate intervention due to life-threatening Addisonian crisis with hemodynamic collapse and severe hypoglycemia. This patient is at risk for cardiovascular collapse and death without emergent IV hydrocortisone and fluid resuscitation.

options: Diabetes insipidus, where free water loss concentrates serum sodium
SIADH, where water retention dilutes sodium
Addison disease, where aldosterone deficiency causes sodium wasting
Hypoparathyroidism, which primarily affects calcium balance
q: A nurse is comparing electrolyte disturbances across endocrine disorders. Which condition causes hypernatremia?
rationale: Diabetes insipidus causes hypernatremia because massive free water loss through dilute urine concentrates serum sodium. In contrast, SIADH causes hyponatremia from water retention, and Addison disease causes hyponatremia from aldosterone-mediated sodium wasting.

options: A patient with new-onset hypothyroidism starting levothyroxine
A patient in thyroid storm with temperature 105.6 degrees, heart rate 168, and altered mental status
A patient with Cushing syndrome awaiting diagnostic testing
A patient with stable diabetes insipidus on DDAVP therapy
q: A nurse is prioritizing care for patients with endocrine emergencies. Which patient requires the MOST immediate intervention?
rationale: A patient in thyroid storm with temperature 105.6 degrees, heart rate 168, and altered mental status requires the most immediate intervention because this is a life-threatening hypermetabolic emergency with mortality of 20-30% even with treatment. Immediate administration of PTU, propranolol, and cooling measures is required.

options: levothyroxine for a patient with hypothyroidism and a TSH of 8 mIU/L
DDAVP for a patient with central diabetes insipidus and a sodium of 148 mEq/L
hydrocortisone for a patient with Addison disease who is NPO for surgery
methimazole for a patient with Graves disease who presents with a WBC of 1,800 cells/mm3 and a sore throat, suggesting agranulocytosis
q: A nurse is reviewing medications for patients with endocrine disorders. Which medication-condition pairing requires the nurse to HOLD the medication and notify the provider?
rationale: Methimazole must be held in a patient with a WBC of 1,800 cells/mm3 and sore throat because these findings suggest agranulocytosis, a life-threatening complication of antithyroid drugs. Continuing methimazole with critically low white blood cells can result in fatal overwhelming infection.
